content.text: When a primitive root exists, we can define a discrete analog of logarithms.

content.title: Index
content.content:
Let $g$ be a primitive root modulo $n$ and $\gcd(a, n) = 1$. The 
**index** of $a$ to the base $g$, denoted $\text{ind}_g(a)$, is 
the unique integer $k$ with $0 \leq k < \phi(n)$ and $g^k \equiv a \pmod{n}$.

content.text: The index is also called the **discrete logarithm**.

content.title: Index Laws
content.content:
Let $g$ be a primitive root modulo $n$. For $\gcd(a, n) = \gcd(b, n) = 1$:

1. $\text{ind}_g(ab) \equiv \text{ind}_g(a) + \text{ind}_g(b) \pmod{\phi(n)}$

2. $\text{ind}_g(a^k) \equiv k \cdot \text{ind}_g(a) \pmod{\phi(n)}$

3. $\text{ind}_g(1) = 0$

4. $\text{ind}_g(g) = 1$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) $g^{\text{ind}_g(a)} \equiv a$ and $g^{\text{ind}_g(b)} \equiv b$, 
so $g^{\text{ind}_g(a) + \text{ind}_g(b)} \equiv ab$. By uniqueness of 
index, $\text{ind}_g(ab) \equiv \text{ind}_g(a) + \text{ind}_g(b)$.

content.text: These laws mirror the laws of ordinary logarithms!

content.title: Solving Using Indices
content.solution:
Taking indices (base 3):
$\text{ind}_3(3) + 5 \cdot \text{ind}_3(x) \equiv \text{ind}_3(4) \pmod{6}$.

$1 + 5 \cdot \text{ind}_3(x) \equiv 4 \pmod{6}$.

$5 \cdot \text{ind}_3(x) \equiv 3 \pmod{6}$.

Since $\gcd(5, 6) = 1$, this has a unique solution. 
$5 \cdot 5 = 25 \equiv 1 \pmod{6}$, so $\text{ind}_3(x) \equiv 5 \cdot 3 = 15 \equiv 3 \pmod{6}$.

From the table, $x \equiv 6 \pmod{7}$.

Check: $3 \cdot 6^5 = 3 \cdot 7776 = 23328$. $23328 / 7 = 3332 + 4/7$, 
so $23328 \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$ $\checkmark$

content.title: Discrete Logarithm Problem
content.content:
Given a prime $p$, a primitive root $g$, and an element $a$, find 
$\text{ind}_g(a)$.

content.text:
For small $p$, this is easy (just compute powers of $g$). For large $p$, 
it is believed to be computationally hard. This hardness is the basis 
of Diffie-Hellman key exchange and related cryptographic protocols.
